sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Faecal incontinence in adults: management — Recommendations
sources.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE pathway adaptation
sources.population: Adults reporting faecal incontinence; neurological, obstetric, postoperative, prolapse, cancer-warning, and acute spinal contexts need tailored assessment.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and specialist care as qualified by the cited recommendations.
sources.applicability_note: Supports physical, emotional, social, bowel, medical, cognitive, and clinician-performed anorectal assessment documentation without defaulting an intimate examination or generating treatment.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.2.3 — impact and baseline assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports relevant medical history, contributory factors, physical and emotional impact, general examination, anorectal examination only if performed, cognition, and warning context.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.1–1.6.1 — review and specialist context
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-entered symptom review, prior management, specialist assessment status, preferences, dignity, and follow-up documentation.
